skill.name: conflict-resolver
skill.description: Analyze Git merge conflicts, compare branch intent, suggest safe resolutions, and recommend validation steps after merging.
skill.summary: Resolve Git merge conflicts with context-aware strategy guidance and post-merge validation.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: AI review found no prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, or installed executable payloads. Most static findings are false positives from fenced Markdown examples, but the skill contains Git and shell guidance that can modify a repository if executed without confirmation.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Modifying Git Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documents git checkout, git add, git clean, git rm, and merge continuation commands. These commands are legitimate for conflict resolution, but they can discard local changes, remove files, or stage repository content if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain explicit Git commands that alter working tree or merge state. The surrounding context is normal conflict-resolution guidance, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Automated Shell Scripts Can Change Conflict Files
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The auto-resolution script loops over conflicted files and chooses ours or theirs based on diff output, then stages files. This could produce incorrect resolutions or discard one side of a conflict if copied and run blindly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script contains a real bash workflow with git checkout and git add operations. It is presented as an example script, not hidden code, which reduces malicious-intent confidence.

low_findings.title: Illustrative Network and Credential Snippets
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The fetch calls and bearer token text are sample application code inside a merge conflict example. No evidence found that the skill sends data to an external endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls appear inside fenced JavaScript examples that demonstrate conflict resolution. They do not define executable skill behavior or an author-controlled destination.

low_findings.title: False Positive Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Matches
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The static hits map to ordinary prose and example error handling. No evidence found of weak cryptographic algorithms, system reconnaissance, or network reconnaissance behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows descriptive text, conflict-resolution notes, or sample validation code. There is no semantic evidence of cryptography or reconnaissance intent.

low_findings.title: Database References Are Example Application Code
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The db.users and db.profiles references are TypeScript conflict examples. No evidence found that the skill accesses SQLite database files or user databases.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited references are inside fenced code examples for class method conflicts. They are not file paths, database connection logic, or runnable skill code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Fenced Bash Examples with Git Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes many bash snippets that can alter Git state. They are visible documentation examples, but agents should request confirmation before executing repository-modifying commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly visible in Markdown bash blocks. The code is not hidden, but its commands can modify a repository.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Substitution in Example Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The auto-resolution example uses shell command substitution to count remaining conflicts. This is normal bash, but it confirms the example is executable if copied into a shell.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitution is explicit on the cited line. It is low-risk by itself but appears inside a script that can change Git state.

content.value_statement: Merge conflicts slow development and can hide subtle regressions. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code analyze both sides, choose a strategy, and validate the result.
actual_capabilities: Classifies content, structural, semantic, and whitespace conflicts.
Explains current and incoming changes using conflict markers and branch intent.
Recommends ours, theirs, manual, or combined resolution strategies.
Provides Git command workflows for status checks, merge tools, and staging.
Suggests validation steps such as linting, tests, builds, and marker checks.
Describes prevention practices including frequent rebases and smaller commits.
limitations: It cannot guarantee semantic correctness without project-specific tests.
Automatic resolution guidance can discard changes if applied without review.
Binary file conflicts still require a human choice between versions.
It does not replace coordination with authors for complex product decisions.

use_cases.title: Feature Developer Resolving a Rebase
use_cases.description: Compare conflicting changes, keep both feature intent paths, and plan verification after the rebase.

use_cases.title: Reviewer Checking a Manual Merge
use_cases.description: Review chosen resolutions for missing behavior, leftover markers, and test coverage gaps.

use_cases.title: Team Lead Planning Conflict Reduction
use_cases.description: Identify recurring conflict patterns and recommend branch, commit, and communication practices.

prompt_templates.title: Explain This Conflict
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner analysis of one conflicted file.
prompt_templates.prompt: @conflict-resolver Analyze this merge conflict. Explain what each side changed, what risk each option has, and which resolution you recommend.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Resolution Strategy
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate decision support for multiple conflicts.
prompt_templates.prompt: @conflict-resolver Review these conflicted files and group them by resolution strategy: ours, theirs, combine, or manual review.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Completed Merge
prompt_templates.prompt: @conflict-resolver Validate this merge result. Look for missed intent, remaining conflict markers, syntax risk, and tests I should run.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Complex Refactor Merge
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced guidance for structural and semantic conflicts.
prompt_templates.prompt: @conflict-resolver Build a conflict resolution plan for a refactor branch merging with a feature branch. Preserve both behaviors and identify manual checkpoints.

output_examples.input: A conflict changes the same import list and also moves one local hook path.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends combining the new import with the existing correct path, then running lint and the affected component tests.

output_examples.input: One branch refactors an authentication service while another adds token refresh behavior.
output_examples.output: The skill explains that the refactored structure should remain, then places the new behavior inside the updated service design.

output_examples.input: A merge includes binary file conflicts and several modified source files.
output_examples.output: The skill separates binary choices from source merges and flags the binary files for human selection before staging.

best_practices: Inspect both branches before choosing ours or theirs.
Run targeted tests after each complex manual resolution.
Ask the original authors when the business intent is unclear.
anti_patterns: Do not run auto-resolution scripts on important branches without a backup.
Do not accept one side of a semantic conflict only because tests pass.
Do not stage every file before reviewing the final merged diff.

faq.question: Can this skill automatically fix every merge conflict?
faq.answer: No. It suggests safe strategies, but complex semantic conflicts need human review and project tests.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Can it handle refactor conflicts?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives patterns for preserving refactors while adding feature changes from another branch.

faq.question: Will it run Git commands by itself?
faq.answer: The skill contains command guidance. Users should confirm any repository-changing command before execution.

faq.question: What should I do after resolving conflicts?
faq.answer: Check for remaining markers, review the final diff, run lint, run tests, and build affected code.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: No malicious exfiltration was found. Still review command output and avoid sharing sensitive code in prompts.
